1→---
     2→title: Workflows
     3→description: All 12 SKF workflows with commands, steps, and connection diagram
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Workflows Reference
     7→
     8→SKF has 12 workflows. You trigger them by typing commands to [Ferris](../agents.md), the AI agent that runs everything. Each workflow handles a specific part of the skill lifecycle — from analyzing source code to packaging for distribution. If any terms are unfamiliar, see the [Concepts](../concepts.md) page for plain-English definitions.
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Core Workflows
    13→
    14→### Setup Forge (SF)
    15→
    16→**Command:** `@Ferris SF`
    17→
    18→**Purpose:** Initialize forge environment, detect tools (ast-grep, ccc, gh, qmd), set capability tier, index project in CCC (Forge+), verify QMD collection health (Deep).
    19→
    20→**When to Use:** First time using SKF in a project. Run once per project.
    21→
    22→**Key Steps:** Detect tools → CCC index check (Forge+) → Determine tier → Write forge-tier.yaml → QMD hygiene (Deep)
    23→
    24→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→### Brief Skill (BS)
    29→
    30→**Command:** `@Ferris BS`
    31→
    32→**Purpose:** Scope and design a skill through guided discovery.
    33→
    34→**When to Use:** Before `Create Skill` when you want maximum control over what gets compiled.
    35→
    36→**Key Steps:** Gather intent → Analyze target → Define scope → Scripts/assets intent → Write skill-brief.yaml
    37→
    38→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→### Create Skill (CS)
    43→
    44→**Command:** `@Ferris CS`
    45→
    46→**Purpose:** Compile a skill from a brief. Supports `--batch` for multiple briefs.
    47→
    48→**When to Use:** After Brief Skill, or with an existing skill-brief.yaml.
    49→
    50→**Key Steps:** Load brief → Ecosystem check → AST extract → Scripts/assets detect → QMD enrich → Compile → Validate → Generate
    51→
    52→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→### Update Skill (US)
    57→
    58→**Command:** `@Ferris US`
    59→
    60→**Purpose:** Smart regeneration preserving `[MANUAL]` sections. Detects individual vs stack internally.
    61→
    62→**When to Use:** After source code changes when an existing skill needs updating.
    63→
    64→**Key Steps:** Load existing → Detect changes (incl. scripts/assets) → Re-extract → Merge (preserve MANUAL) → Validate → Write
    65→
    66→**Agent:** Ferris (Surgeon mode)
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## Feature Workflows
    71→
    72→### Quick Skill (QS)
    73→
    74→**Command:** `@Ferris QS <package-or-url>`
    75→
    76→**Purpose:** Brief-less fast skill with package-to-repo resolution.
    77→
    78→**When to Use:** When you need a skill quickly — no brief needed. Accepts package names or GitHub URLs.
    79→
    80→**Key Steps:** Resolve target → Ecosystem check → Quick extract → Compile → Validate → Write
    81→
    82→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→### Stack Skill (SS)
    87→
    88→**Command:** `@Ferris SS`
    89→
    90→**Purpose:** Consolidated project stack skill with integration patterns.
    91→
    92→**When to Use:** When you want your agent to understand your entire project stack — not just individual libraries.
    93→
    94→**Key Steps:** Detect manifests → Rank dependencies → Scope confirmation → Parallel extract → Detect integrations → Compile stack → Generate references
    95→
    96→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→### Analyze Source (AN)
   101→
   102→**Command:** `@Ferris AN`
   103→
   104→**Purpose:** Decomposition engine — discover what to skill, recommend stack skill.
   105→
   106→**When to Use:** Brownfield onboarding of large repos or multi-service projects.
   107→
   108→**Key Steps:** Scan project → Identify units → Map exports → Detect integrations → Recommend → Generate briefs
   109→
   110→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
   111→
   112→---
   113→
   114→### Audit Skill (AS)
   115→
   116→**Command:** `@Ferris AS`
   117→
   118→**Purpose:** Drift detection between skill and current source.
   119→
   120→**When to Use:** To check if a skill has fallen out of date with its source code.
   121→
   122→**Key Steps:** Load skill → Re-index source → Structural diff → Script/asset drift → Semantic diff (Deep) → Classify severity → Report
   123→
   124→**Agent:** Ferris (Audit mode)
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→### Test Skill (TS)
   129→
   130→**Command:** `@Ferris TS`
   131→
   132→**Purpose:** Cognitive completeness verification. Quality gate before export.
   133→
   134→**When to Use:** After creating or updating a skill, before exporting.
   135→
   136→**Key Steps:** Load skill → Detect mode → Coverage check → Coherence check → Score → Gap report
   137→
   138→**Agent:** Ferris (Audit mode)
   139→
   140→---
   141→
   142→## Architecture Verification Workflows
   143→
   144→### Verify Stack (VS)
   145→
   146→**Command:** `@Ferris VS`
   147→
   148→**Purpose:** Pre-code stack feasibility verification. Cross-references generated skills against architecture and PRD documents with three passes: coverage, integration compatibility, and requirements.
   149→
   150→**When to Use:** After generating individual skills with CS/QS, before building a stack skill — to verify the tech stack can support the architecture.
   151→
   152→**Key Steps:** Load skills + docs → Coverage analysis → Integration verification → Requirements check → Synthesize verdict → Present report
   153→
   154→**Agent:** Ferris (Audit mode)
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Refine Architecture (RA)
   159→
   160→**Command:** `@Ferris RA`
   161→
   162→**Purpose:** Evidence-backed architecture improvement. Takes the original architecture doc + generated skills + optional VS report, fills gaps, flags contradictions, and suggests improvements — all citing specific APIs.
   163→
   164→**When to Use:** After VS confirms feasibility, before running SS in compose-mode. Produces a refined architecture ready for stack skill composition.
   165→
   166→**Key Steps:** Load inputs → Gap analysis → Issue detection → Improvement detection → Compile refined doc → Present report
   167→
   168→**Agent:** Ferris (Architect mode)
   169→
   170→---
   171→
   172→## Utility Workflows
   173→
   174→### Export Skill (EX)
   175→
   176→**Command:** `@Ferris EX`
   177→
   178→**Purpose:** Package for distribution + platform-aware context injection into CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md.
   179→
   180→**When to Use:** When a skill is ready for distribution or CLAUDE.md integration.
   181→
   182→**Key Steps:** Load skill → Package → Generate snippet → Update CLAUDE.md → Token report → Summary
   183→
   184→**Agent:** Ferris (Delivery mode)
   185→
   186→---
   187→
   188→## Workflow Connections
   189→
   190→```
   191→                    setup-forge (one-time)
   192→                         │
   193→          ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
   194→          │              │              │
   195→     analyze-source  quick-skill  create-stack-skill (code-mode)
   196→          │              │              │
   197→     brief-skill         │              │
   198→          │              │              │
   199→     create-skill        │              │
   200→          │              │              │
   201→          └──────┬───────┘──────────────┘
   202→                 │
   203→            test-skill (quality gate)
   204→                 │
   205→          ┌──────┼──────┐
   206→          │             │
   207→     export-skill  audit-skill
   208→                        │
   209→                   update-skill (loops back to test)
   210→
   211→
   212→  Pre-code verification path (compose-mode):
   213→
   214→     (create-skill | quick-skill) ×N (per library)
   215→          │
   216→     verify-stack (VS) ── feasibility report (optional, recommended)
   217→          │
   218→     refine-architecture (RA) ── refined architecture doc (VS report optional)
   219→          │
   220→     create-stack-skill (SS compose-mode)
   221→          │
   222→     test-skill → export-skill
   223→```
   224→
   225→---
   226→
   227→## Build Priority
   228→
   229→| Phase | Workflows | Notes |
   230→|-------|-----------|-------|
   231→| 1a (Core Engine) | setup-forge, create-skill, test-skill, export-skill | Must build first |
   232→| 1b (Entry Points) | quick-skill, brief-skill, create-stack-skill | Complete v1.0 |
   233→| 1c (Verification) | verify-stack, refine-architecture | Pre-code architecture verification |
   234→| 2 (Lifecycle) | audit-skill, update-skill, analyze-source | Post-launch |
   235→

1→# SKF Workflows
     2→
     3→Twelve workflows covering the full skill lifecycle — from source discovery through verified export, with pre-code architecture verification.
     4→
     5→All workflows are triggered via Ferris (`@Ferris <code>`) and execute as step-by-step sequences with just-in-time step loading.
     6→
     7→## Workflow Index
     8→
     9→| Code | Workflow | Steps | Mode | Purpose |
    10→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
    11→| SF | [setup-forge](setup-forge/workflow.md) | 4 | Architect | Initialize environment, detect tools, set capability tier |
    12→| AN | [analyze-source](analyze-source/workflow.md) | 7 | Architect | Discover skillable units in a repo, produce recommended briefs |
    13→| BS | [brief-skill](brief-skill/workflow.md) | 5 | Architect | Design a skill scope through guided discovery |
    14→| CS | [create-skill](create-skill/workflow.md) | 8 | Architect | Compile a skill from brief with provenance (supports --batch) |
    15→| QS | [quick-skill](quick-skill/workflow.md) | 6 | Architect | Fast skill from package name or GitHub URL — no brief needed |
    16→| SS | [create-stack-skill](create-stack-skill/workflow.md) | 9 | Architect | Consolidated project stack skill with integration patterns (code-mode + compose-mode) |
    17→| VS | [verify-stack](verify-stack/workflow.md) | 6 | Audit | Validate tech stack feasibility against architecture and PRD |
    18→| RA | [refine-architecture](refine-architecture/workflow.md) | 6 | Architect | Improve architecture doc using verified skill data |
    19→| US | [update-skill](update-skill/workflow.md) | 7 | Surgeon | Regenerate after source changes, preserving \[MANUAL\] sections |
    20→| AS | [audit-skill](audit-skill/workflow.md) | 6 | Audit | Drift detection between skill and current source code |
    21→| TS | [test-skill](test-skill/workflow.md) | 6 | Audit | Cognitive completeness verification — quality gate before export |
    22→| EX | [export-skill](export-skill/workflow.md) | 6 | Delivery | Package for distribution, inject into CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md |
    23→
    24→## Typical Flows
    25→
    26→**First-time setup:**
    27→
    28→```
    29→SF → AN → CS (batch) → TS → EX
    30→```
    31→
    32→**Single skill (full quality):**
    33→
    34→```
    35→SF → BS → CS → TS → EX
    36→```
    37→
    38→**Single skill (fast):**
    39→
    40→```
    41→SF → QS → EX
    42→```
    43→
    44→**Pre-code stack verification:**
    45→
    46→```
    47→SF → CS×N (per library) → VS → RA → SS (compose) → TS → EX
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Maintenance:**
    51→
    52→```
    53→AS → US → TS → EX
    54→```
    55→
    56→## Knowledge vs. Data
    57→
    58→The module uses two kinds of reference material, loaded just-in-time during workflow execution:
    59→
    60→| Aspect | Knowledge (`knowledge/`) | Data (`{workflow}/data/`) |
    61→| --- | --- | --- |
    62→| Scope | Cross-cutting, multi-workflow | Single workflow, single step |
    63→| Content | Principles, patterns, judgment rules | Schemas, templates, scoring formulas |
    64→| Index | `skf-knowledge-index.csv` | Referenced directly by step files |
    65→| Examples | zero-hallucination, confidence-tiers | skill-brief-schema.md, scoring-rules.md |
    66→
    67→**Knowledge files** live in `src/knowledge/` and capture principles Ferris applies across multiple workflows — zero hallucination, confidence tiers, provenance tracking, \[MANUAL\] preservation. Workflow steps reference these when they need cross-cutting judgment, not step-specific rules.
    68→
    69→**Data files** live in each workflow's `data/` directory and contain operational artifacts tightly coupled to specific steps — schemas, templates, scoring formulas, extraction patterns.
    70→
    71→Neither duplicates the other. Knowledge files reference (not repeat) data files where the detailed operational rules live.
    72→
    73→## Directory Structure
    74→
    75→Each workflow follows a consistent structure:
    76→
    77→```
    78→{workflow}/
    79→├── workflow.md              # Entry point — goals, mode routing, step sequence
    80→├── steps-c/                 # Create-mode steps (sequential execution)
    81→│   ├── step-01-*.md
    82→│   ├── step-02-*.md
    83→│   └── ...
    84→├── data/                    # Reference data — schemas, rules, patterns
    85→│   └── *.md
    86→├── templates/               # Output skeletons (only analyze-source, test-skill)
    87→│   └── *-template.md
    88→├── workflow-plan-*.md       # Design plan (build artifact)
    89→└── validation-report.md     # BMAD validation results (build artifact)
    90→```
    91→
    92→### Step Loading
    93→
    94→Steps execute sequentially via just-in-time loading. Only the current step is in context. Each step explicitly names the next step file. Data and template files are loaded on demand by the steps that need them.
    95→
    96→## Workflow Details
    97→
    98→### setup-forge (SF)
    99→
   100→**4 steps** | **Data:** tier-rules.md
   101→
   102→Detects available tools (ast-grep, gh, QMD), determines the capability tier (Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep), writes `forge-tier.yaml` to the sidecar, and optionally indexes the project. Run this first — all other workflows adapt behavior to the detected tier.
   103→
   104→### analyze-source (AN)
   105→
   106→**7 steps** | **Data:** skill-brief-schema.md, unit-detection-heuristics.md | **Templates:** analysis-report-template.md
   107→
   108→Brownfield onboarding entry point. Scans project structure, identifies skillable units using confidence-tiered heuristic signals, maps exports and integration points, presents recommendations for user confirmation, then generates `skill-brief.yaml` files ready for `create-skill --batch`.
   109→
   110→### brief-skill (BS)
   111→
   112→**5 steps** | **Data:** skill-brief-schema.md
   113→
   114→Interactive guided discovery. Gathers user intent (target repo, language, what to skill), analyzes the target to understand boundaries, walks through scope definition with the user, confirms the brief, then writes a validated `skill-brief.yaml`.
   115→
   116→### create-skill (CS)
   117→
   118→**8 steps** | **Data:** extraction-patterns.md, skill-sections.md
   119→
   120→The core compilation engine. Loads a brief, checks ecosystem context, extracts source with AST-backed provenance (T1 confidence when ast-grep available), enriches with usage patterns, compiles into agentskills.io-compliant sections, validates structural integrity, generates the `SKILL.md` artifact, and produces an evidence report. Supports `--batch` for processing multiple briefs.
   121→
   122→### quick-skill (QS)
   123→
   124→**6 steps** | **Data:** registry-resolution.md, skill-template.md
   125→
   126→Fastest path to a skill. Accepts a GitHub URL or package name, resolves to source via registry lookup, performs ecosystem check, extracts public API surface without requiring a brief, compiles best-effort `SKILL.md`, validates, and writes output.
   127→
   128→### create-stack-skill (SS)
   129→
   130→**9 steps** | **Data:** integration-patterns.md, manifest-patterns.md, stack-skill-template.md, compose-mode-rules.md
   131→
   132→Two modes: **Code-mode** analyzes dependency manifests and co-import patterns from actual source code. **Compose-mode** synthesizes a stack skill from pre-generated individual skills + architecture document (no codebase required). Both produce the same output format — consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.
   133→
   134→### verify-stack (VS)
   135→
   136→**6 steps** | **Data:** integration-verification-rules.md, coverage-patterns.md, feasibility-report-template.md
   137→
   138→Pre-code verification engine. Cross-references generated skills against architecture and PRD documents. Three passes: coverage verification (does a skill exist for each tech?), integration verification (do the APIs actually connect?), and requirements verification (does the stack cover the PRD?). Produces a feasibility report with evidence-backed verdicts and prescriptive recommendations. Re-runnable — swap libraries, regenerate skills, re-verify.
   139→
   140→### refine-architecture (RA)
   141→
   142→**6 steps** | **Data:** refinement-rules.md
   143→
   144→Architecture improvement engine. Takes the original architecture document and refines it using verified skill data and VS feasibility findings. Fills gaps, flags issues, suggests improvements — all backed by specific API evidence from the generated skills. Produces a refined architecture document.
   145→
   146→### update-skill (US)
   147→
   148→**7 steps** | **Data:** manual-section-rules.md, merge-conflict-rules.md
   149→
   150→Surgical update engine. Loads the existing skill, detects what changed in source since last compilation, re-extracts only affected exports with fresh provenance, merges new extractions while preserving all `[MANUAL]` developer-authored content, validates the merged result, writes the updated skill, and produces a change report.
   151→
   152→### audit-skill (AS)
   153→
   154→**6 steps** | **Data:** drift-report-template.md, severity-rules.md
   155→
   156→Drift detection engine. Loads the skill under audit, re-indexes current source to build a fresh reference, performs structural diff (signature changes, missing exports), performs semantic diff (behavioral divergence), classifies each finding by severity using defined rules, and produces a graded drift report.
   157→
   158→### test-skill (TS)
   159→
   160→**6 steps** | **Data:** output-section-formats.md, scoring-rules.md | **Templates:** test-report-template.md
   161→
   162→Quality gate before export. Loads the skill, detects test mode (naive for Quick tier, contextual for Forge/Deep), runs coverage checks against source, runs coherence checks for internal consistency, scores using weighted rubric, and produces a completeness report with pass/fail gate decision.
   163→
   164→### export-skill (EX)
   165→
   166→**6 steps** | **Data:** managed-section-format.md, snippet-format.md
   167→
   168→Distribution packaging. Loads the completed skill, packages as agentskills.io-compliant bundle, generates context snippets optimized for token efficiency, updates managed sections in `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` with platform-aware injection, produces a token budget report, and summarizes the export.
   169→

     5→
     6→# Step 8: Report
     7→
     8→## STEP GOAL:
     9→
    10→To display the final compilation summary — skill name, version, source, export count, confidence distribution, tier used, file list, and any warnings — and suggest next steps for the user.
    11→
    12→## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):
    13→
    14→### Universal Rules:
    15→
    16→- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
    17→- 🎯 ALWAYS follow the exact instructions in the step file
    18→- ⚙️ TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK: If any instruction references a tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    19→- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    20→
    21→### Role Reinforcement:
    22→
    23→- ✅ You are a skill compilation engine delivering the final report
    24→- ✅ This is the completion step — celebrate the forged skill
    25→- ✅ Positive capability framing — describe what was achieved
    26→
    27→### Step-Specific Rules:
    28→
    29→- 🎯 Focus ONLY on reporting compilation results
    30→- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to modify any files — reporting only
    31→- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to re-run extraction or compilation
    32→- 💬 Deliver structured report with confidence breakdown
    33→
    34→## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:
    35→
    36→- 🎯 Follow MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
    37→- 💾 No file operations — display only
    38→- 📖 This is the final step — no next step to load
    39→- 🚫 Do not modify any output files
    40→
    41→## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:
    42→
    43→- Available: All data from previous steps — brief, extraction, enrichment, compilation, validation, artifacts
    44→- Focus: Summarizing and reporting
    45→- Limits: Do NOT modify files or re-run any previous step
    46→- Dependencies: All artifacts must have been generated in step-07
    47→
    48→## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
    49→
    50→**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.
    51→
    52→### 1. Display Forge Completion Banner
    53→
    54→"**Skill forged: {name} v{version} — {export_count} functions, {primary_confidence} confidence.**"
    55→
    56→Where `{primary_confidence}` is the predominant confidence tier (T1 if Forge/Deep, T1-low if Quick).
    57→
    58→### 2. Display Compilation Summary
    59→
    60→"**Compilation Summary**
    61→
    62→| Field | Value |
    63→|-------|-------|
    64→| **Skill** | {name} v{version} |
    65→| **Source** | {source_repo} @ {branch} ({commit_short}) |
    66→| **Language** | {language} |
    67→| **Forge Tier** | {tier} — {tier_description} |
    68→| **Files Scanned** | {file_count} |
    69→| **Exports Documented** | {documented_count} public API ({public_api_coverage}%) / {total_count} total ({total_coverage}%) |
    70→
    71→**Confidence Distribution:**
    72→| Tier | Count | Description |
    73→|------|-------|-------------|
    74→| T1 (AST) | {t1_count} | Structurally verified via ast-grep |
    75→| T1-low (Source) | {t1_low_count} | Inferred from source reading |
    76→| T2 (QMD) | {t2_count} | Enriched with temporal context |
    77→| T3 (External) | {t3_count} | Sourced from external documentation URLs |
    78→
    79→**Output Files:**
    80→- `skills/{name}/SKILL.md` — Active skill with trigger-based usage
    81→- `skills/{name}/context-snippet.md` — Passive context for CLAUDE.md
    82→- `skills/{name}/metadata.json` — Machine-readable birth certificate
    83→- `skills/{name}/references/` — Progressive disclosure ({ref_count} files)
    84→- `forge-data/{name}/provenance-map.json` — Source map with AST bindings
    85→- `forge-data/{name}/evidence-report.md` — Build audit trail
    86→- `forge-data/{name}/extraction-rules.yaml` — Reproducible extraction schema"
    87→
    88→### 3. Display Warnings (If Any)
    89→
    90→If there were warnings from extraction, validation, or enrichment, display them:
    91→
    92→"**Warnings:**
    93→- {warning_1}
    94→- {warning_2}
    95→- ..."
    96→
    97→If no warnings, omit this section entirely.
    98→
    99→### 4. Suggest Next Steps
   100→
   101→"**Recommended next steps:**
   102→- **[TS] Test Skill** — verify completeness and accuracy before export
   103→- **[EX] Export Skill** — publish to your skill library or agentskills.io
   104→- **[US] Update Skill** — edit specific sections or add manual content
   105→
   106→To use this skill immediately, add the context snippet to your CLAUDE.md:
   107→```
   108→{context_snippet_content}
   109→```"
   110→
   111→### 5. Batch Mode Status (If Applicable)
   112→
   113→**If running in --batch mode:**
   114→
   115→"**Batch progress:** {completed_count} of {total_count} skills compiled.
   116→
   117→{If more remaining:} Proceeding to next brief: {next_skill_name}..."
   118→
   119→Update sidecar checkpoint with batch progress, then loop back to step-01 for the next brief.
   120→
   121→**If not batch mode or all batch briefs complete:**
   122→
   123→End workflow. No further steps.
   124→
   125→### 6. Workflow Complete
   126→
   127→This is the final step. No `{nextStepFile}` to load.
   128→
   129→Display: "Forge session complete. Return to Ferris menu for further actions."
   130→
   131→## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
   132→
   133→This is the FINAL STEP of the create-skill workflow. After displaying the compilation report and next steps, the workflow is complete. No further steps are loaded.
   134→
   135→For batch mode: loop back to step-01 for remaining briefs via sidecar checkpoint.
   136→
   137→---
   138→
   139→## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS
   140→
   141→### ✅ SUCCESS:
   142→
   143→- Forge completion banner displayed with skill name, version, export count
   144→- Full compilation summary with confidence distribution
   145→- All output file paths listed
   146→- Warnings displayed (if any)
   147→- Next steps suggested (test-skill, export-skill, update-skill)
   148→- Context snippet provided for immediate use
   149→- Batch progress updated (if applicable)
   150→- Workflow ended gracefully
   151→
   152→### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:
   153→
   154→- Not displaying the forge completion banner
   155→- Missing confidence distribution in summary
   156→- Modifying any files during the report step
   157→- Not listing all output file paths
   158→- Not suggesting next steps
   159→- Not ending the workflow (attempting to load nonexistent next step)
   160→
   161→**Master Rule:** This step reports and celebrates. It does not modify, re-extract, or re-compile. The skill is forged.
   162→

     4→nextStepFile: './step-02-ecosystem-check.md'

     8→
     9→# Step 1: Load Brief
    10→
    11→## STEP GOAL:
    12→
    13→To load and validate the skill-brief.yaml compilation config, resolve the source code location, and load the forge tier from sidecar to determine available capabilities for the compilation pipeline.
    14→
    15→## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):
    16→
    17→### Universal Rules:
    18→
    19→- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
    20→- 🎯 ALWAYS follow the exact instructions in the step file
    21→- ⚙️ TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK: If any instruction references a tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    22→- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    23→
    24→### Role Reinforcement:
    25→
    26→- ✅ You are a skill compilation engine performing initialization
    27→- ✅ This is an autonomous init step — no user interaction needed
    28→- ✅ Load data, validate structure, resolve paths, determine capabilities
    29→
    30→### Step-Specific Rules:
    31→
    32→- 🎯 Focus ONLY on loading brief, resolving source, and determining tier
    33→- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to begin extraction or compilation — that's steps 03-05
    34→- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to write any output files — this step only loads and validates
    35→- 💬 Report initialization status clearly
    36→
    37→## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:
    38→
    39→- 🎯 Follow MANDATORY SEQUENCE exactly
    40→- 💾 All loaded data remains in context for subsequent steps
    41→- 📖 Validate brief structure before proceeding
    42→- 🚫 Halt with actionable error if prerequisites are missing
    43→
    44→## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:
    45→
    46→- Available: User's skill name or brief path from Ferris menu invocation
    47→- Focus: Loading inputs and determining capabilities
    48→- Limits: Do NOT begin any extraction or compilation work
    49→- Dependencies: forge-tier.yaml must exist (from setup-forge workflow)
    50→
    51→## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
    52→
    53→**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.
    54→
    55→### 1. Load Forge Tier
    56→
    57→Load `{forgeTierFile}` completely.
    58→
    59→**If file does not exist:**
    60→Halt with: "Forge halted: No forge configuration found. Run [SF] Setup Forge first to detect tools and set your tier."
    61→
    62→**If file exists:**
    63→Extract and report:
    64→- `tier`: Quick, Forge, Forge+, or Deep
    65→- `tools`: which tools are available (gh, ast-grep, ccc, qmd)
    66→- `ccc_index`: ccc index state (status, indexed_path, last_indexed) — needed by step-02b
    67→
    68→**Apply tier override:** Read `{preferencesFile}`. If `tier_override` is set and is a valid tier value (Quick, Forge, Forge+, or Deep), use it instead of the detected tier.
    69→
    70→### 2. Discover Skill Brief
    71→
    72→**If user provided a specific brief path or skill name:**
    73→- Search `{project-root}/forge-data/{skill-name}/skill-brief.yaml`
    74→- If found, load it completely
    75→
    76→**If user invoked with --batch flag:**
    77→- Search specified directory for all `skill-brief.yaml` files
    78→- List discovered briefs with skill names
    79→- Store list for batch loop processing
    80→- For this run, load the FIRST brief (batch loops back for remaining)
    81→
    82→**If no brief found:**
    83→Halt with: "No skill brief found. Run [BS] Brief Skill to create one, or use [QS] Quick Skill for brief-less generation."
    84→
    85→### 3. Validate Brief Structure
    86→
    87→Check that the loaded skill-brief.yaml contains required fields:
    88→
    89→**Required fields:**
    90→- `name` — skill identifier (kebab-case)
    91→- `version` — source version to compile against
    92→- `source_repo` — GitHub owner/repo or local path (**optional when `source_type: "docs-only"`**)
    93→- `language` — primary source language
    94→- `scope` — what to extract (e.g., "all public exports", specific modules)
    95→
    96→**Optional fields:**
    97→- `source_type` — `"source"` (default) or `"docs-only"` (external documentation only)
    98→- `doc_urls` — array of `{url, label}` documentation URLs (required when `source_type: "docs-only"`)
    99→- `source_branch` — branch to use (default: main/master)
   100→- `source_authority` — official/community/internal (default: community; forced to `community` for docs-only)
   101→- `include_patterns` — file glob patterns to include
   102→- `exclude_patterns` — file glob patterns to exclude
   103→- `description` — human description of the skill
   104→
   105→**Docs-only validation:** When `source_type: "docs-only"`, `source_repo` is not required but `doc_urls` must have at least one entry. `source_authority` is forced to `community`.
   106→
   107→**If required fields missing:**
   108→Halt with specific error: "Brief validation failed: missing required field `{field}`. Update your skill-brief.yaml and re-run."
   109→
   110→### 4. Resolve Source Code Location
   111→
   112→**If source_repo is a GitHub URL or owner/repo format:**
   113→- Verify repository exists via `gh_bridge.list_tree(owner, repo, branch)`
   114→- If branch not specified, detect default branch
   115→- Store resolved: owner, repo, branch, file tree
   116→
   117→**If source_repo is a local path:**
   118→- Verify path exists and contains source files
   119→- Store resolved: local path, file listing
   120→
   121→**If source cannot be resolved:**
   122→Halt with: "Source not found: `{source_repo}`. Verify the repository exists and is accessible."
   123→
   124→### 5. Report Initialization
   125→
   126→Display initialization summary:
   127→
   128→"**Forge initialized.**
   129→
   130→**Skill:** {name} v{version}
   131→**Source:** {source_repo} @ {branch}
   132→**Language:** {language}
   133→**Scope:** {scope}
   134→**Tier:** {tier} — {tier_description}
   135→**Tools:** {available_tools_list}
   136→
   137→Proceeding to ecosystem check..."
   138→
   139→Where tier_description follows positive capability framing:
   140→- Quick: "Source reading and spec validation"
   141→- Forge: "AST-backed structural extraction"
   142→- Forge+: "Semantic-guided precision — ccc pre-ranks files before AST extraction"
   143→- Deep: "Full intelligence — structural + contextual + temporal"
   144→
   145→### 6. Menu Handling Logic
   146→
   147→**Auto-proceed step — no user interaction.**
   148→
   149→After initialization is complete and all data is loaded, immediately load, read entire file, then execute `{nextStepFile}`.
   150→
   151→#### EXECUTION RULES:
   152→
   153→- This is an auto-proceed initialization step with no user choices
   154→- Proceed directly to next step after successful initialization
   155→- If any prerequisite check fails, HALT with actionable error — do NOT proceed
   156→
   157→## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
   158→
   159→ONLY WHEN forge-tier.yaml is loaded, skill-brief.yaml is validated, and source code location is resolved will you proceed to load `{nextStepFile}` for ecosystem check.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS
   164→
   165→### ✅ SUCCESS:
   166→
   167→- Forge tier loaded from sidecar with tool availability
   168→- Skill brief loaded and all required fields validated
   169→- Source code location resolved and accessible
   170→- Initialization summary displayed with tier and capabilities
   171→- Auto-proceeded to step-02
   172→
   173→### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:
   174→
   175→- Proceeding without forge-tier.yaml (missing prerequisite)
   176→- Proceeding with invalid or incomplete brief
   177→- Not resolving source code location before proceeding
   178→- Beginning extraction or compilation work in this step
   179→- Not displaying initialization summary
   180→
   181→**Master Rule:** This step ONLY loads and validates. It does NOT extract, compile, or write files.
   182→

     3→description: 'Display final stack skill summary with confidence distribution and next workflow recommendations'
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Step 9: Stack Skill Report
     7→
     8→## STEP GOAL:
     9→
    10→Display the final summary of the forged stack skill with confidence distribution, output file listing, and next workflow recommendations.
    11→
    12→## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):
    13→
    14→### Universal Rules:
    15→
    16→- 📖 CRITICAL: Read the complete step file before taking any action
    17→- ⚙️ TOOL/SUBPROCESS FALLBACK: If any instruction references a subprocess, subagent, or tool you do not have access to, you MUST still achieve the outcome in your main context thread
    18→- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
    19→
    20→### Role Reinforcement:
    21→
    22→- ✅ You are Ferris delivering the final forge report
    23→- ✅ Positive capability framing — lead with what was accomplished
    24→- ✅ Warnings are secondary — present them only if they exist
    25→
    26→### Step-Specific Rules:
    27→
    28→- 🎯 This is the FINAL step — no nextStepFile
    29→- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to write or modify any files — report is console output only
    30→- 💬 Lead with the positive summary, then details, then warnings if any
    31→- 🎯 Recommend next workflows based on what was produced
    32→
    33→## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:
    34→
    35→- 🎯 Compile summary from all previous step results
    36→- 📖 Display report to user — workflow is complete after this
    37→- 🚫 No file writes, no state updates — this is terminal output
    38→
    39→## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:
    40→
    41→- From step 01: project_name, forge_tier
    42→- From step 03: confirmed_dependencies[] count
    43→- From step 04: per_library_extractions[] with confidence tiers
    44→- From step 05: integration_graph with pair count
    45→- From step 07: written_files[] with paths
    46→- From step 08: validation_result with findings
    47→- This step produces: console report (no stored artifacts)
    48→
    49→## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
    50→
    51→**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise.
    52→
    53→### 1. Display Stack Forged Banner
    54→
    55→"**Stack forged: {project_name}-stack — {lib_count} libraries, {integration_count} integration patterns**
    56→
    57→Forge tier: **{tier}**"
    58→
    59→### 2. Display Confidence Distribution
    60→
    61→"**Confidence distribution:**
    62→
    63→| Tier | Count | Description |
    64→|------|-------|-------------|
    65→| T1 | {count} | AST-verified structural extraction |
    66→| T1-low | {count} | Source reading inference |
    67→| T2 | {count} | QMD-enriched temporal context |
    68→
    69→{IF compose_mode:}
    70→*Note: Confidence tiers above are inherited from source skills — they reflect the extraction method used when those skills were originally generated, not the current compose run.*"
    71→
    72→### 3. Display Output File Summary
    73→
    74→"**Output files:**
    75→
    76→**Deliverables** (`{skills_output_folder}/{project_name}-stack/`):
    77→- SKILL.md — Integration patterns, library summaries, conventions
    78→- context-snippet.md — Compressed stack index ({token_estimate} tokens)
    79→- metadata.json — Skill metadata and library registry
    80→- references/ — {lib_count} per-library reference files
    81→{If integrations:} - references/integrations/ — {pair_count} integration pair files
    82→
    83→**Workspace** (`{forge_data_folder}/{project_name}-stack/`):
    84→- provenance-map.json — Extraction source tracking
    85→- evidence-report.md — Evidence and confidence breakdown"
    86→
    87→### 4. Display Validation Summary
    88→
    89→**If validation passed with no findings:**
    90→
    91→"**Validation:** All checks passed"
    92→
    93→**If validation had findings:**
    94→
    95→"**Validation:** {warning_count} warning(s) found
    96→{For each finding:}
    97→- ⚠ {description}"
    98→
    99→### 5. Display Warnings (Conditional)
   100→
   101→**Only display if warnings exist from any step:**
   102→
   103→"**Warnings:**
   104→{For each warning from steps 04, 05, 07, 08:}
   105→- {warning_description}"
   106→
   107→**If no warnings:** Skip this section entirely.
   108→
   109→### 6. Recommend Next Workflows
   110→
   111→"**Next steps:**
   112→- **[TS] test-skill** — Validate the stack skill against its own assertions
   113→- **[EX] export-skill** — Package for distribution or agent loading
   114→
   115→**Workflow complete.**"
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## 🚨 SYSTEM SUCCESS/FAILURE METRICS
   120→
   121→### ✅ SUCCESS:
   122→
   123→- Banner displayed with accurate library and integration counts
   124→- Confidence distribution table with correct tier counts
   125→- All output files listed with descriptions
   126→- Validation summary reflects actual step 08 findings
   127→- Warnings displayed only when they exist
   128→- Next workflow recommendations included
   129→- Positive capability framing throughout
   130→
   131→### ❌ SYSTEM FAILURE:
   132→
   133→- Inaccurate counts in banner or distribution table
   134→- Missing output files from the listing
   135→- Displaying warnings section when no warnings exist
   136→- Writing or modifying files in this step
   137→- Not recommending next workflows
   138→- Negative framing or apologetic tone
   139→
   140→**Master Rule:** Celebrate what was built. Report accurately. Recommend next steps. Workflow complete.
   141→

56→
    57→Apply the following decision logic using findings from all completed passes:
    58→
    59→**Evaluate in order — the first matching condition wins. Do not continue once a verdict is determined.**
    60→
    61→**NOT FEASIBLE (evaluate first):**
    62→- Any integration is **Blocked** → overall verdict is NOT FEASIBLE
    63→- Rationale: a blocked integration represents a fundamental architectural incompatibility
    64→
    65→**CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE (evaluate second):**
    66→- Any technology is **Missing** from coverage (no skill exists) → overall verdict is CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE
    67→- Any integration is **Risky** (but none Blocked) → overall verdict is CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE
    68→- Requirements have any **Not Addressed** items → overall verdict is CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE
    69→- Requirements have any **Partially Fulfilled** items (but none Not Addressed) → overall verdict is CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE
    70→- Rationale: the stack can work but has gaps, risks, or unverified assumptions that must be addressed
    71→
    72→**FEASIBLE (evaluate last):**
    73→- Coverage is 100% (no Missing skills) AND all integrations are Verified or Plausible AND requirements are all Fulfilled (or requirements pass was skipped) AND no Blocked or Risky integrations → overall verdict is FEASIBLE
    74→- Rationale: the stack can support the architecture as described — all requirements fully fulfilled
    75→
    76→Store the verdict for use in the report.
    77→
    78→### 2. Generate Prescriptive Recommendations
    79→
    80→For each non-verified finding across all passes, generate an actionable next step:
    81→
    82→**Missing skill (from Step 02):**
    83→- "Run **[CS] Create Skill** or **[QS] Quick Skill** for `{library_name}`, then re-run **[VS]** to verify coverage."
    84→
    85→**Risky integration (from Step 03):**
    86→- If protocol mismatch → "Consider adding a bridge layer between `{lib_a}` and `{lib_b}` (e.g., HTTP adapter, message queue). Document the bridge in the architecture."
    87→- If type incompatibility → "Add a serialization/conversion layer between `{lib_a}` and `{lib_b}` to resolve the type mismatch identified in their API surfaces."
    88→- If weak evidence → "Add cross-references between `{lib_a}` and `{lib_b}` skills by re-running **[CS]** with integration context."
    89→
    90→**Blocked integration (from Step 03):**
    91→- If language barrier → "Replace `{lib_a}` with a `{lib_b_language}`-compatible alternative, or introduce an IPC/FFI bridge. Redesign the integration path in the architecture document."
    92→- If fundamental incompatibility → "Replace `{blocked_lib}` with `{suggested_alternative}` or redesign the integration path."
    93→
    94→**Not Addressed requirement (from Step 04):**
    95→- "No library in the stack covers `{requirement}`. Evaluate `{category}` libraries that provide this capability, generate a skill, then re-run **[VS]**."
    96→
    97→**Partially Fulfilled requirement (from Step 04):**
    98→- "Gap in `{requirement}`: {what_is_missing}. Consider extending `{contributing_skill}` or adding a dedicated library."
    99→
   100→### 3. Check for Previous Report

The `skfConfigFile` issue was reported in the validation report (as a "must fix") but has apparently already been removed from the actual step-01-init.md. Good. Now let me compile my final report.

---

## Deep Cross-Cutting Consistency Review

Reviewing the last 6 commits covering: [VS] Verify Stack, [RA] Refine Architecture, and [SS] compose-mode, spanning 44 files across docs/, src/agents/, src/knowledge/, src/workflows/, src/module-help.csv, CHANGELOG.md, README.md, and test/.

---

## CRITICAL Issues

### 1. docs/agents.md — VS and RA Missing from Modes Table

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/agents.md` line 37

The Ferris modes table in the agents reference doc is stale and excludes the two new workflows:

```
| **Architect** | Exploratory, structural, assembling | SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS |
| **Surgeon**   | Precise, semantic diffing, preserves [MANUAL] | US |
| **Audit**     | Judgmental, drift reports, completeness scoring | AS, TS |
| **Delivery**  | Packaging, platform-aware, ecosystem-ready | EX |
```

`RA` is missing from the Architect row. `VS` is missing from the Audit row.

The correct table is in `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/how-it-works.md` line 67:
```
| **Architect** | SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, RA | ...
| **Audit**     | AS, TS, VS                 | ...
```

This is a direct contradiction between two documentation files. The agents.md modes table will mislead users about which workflows exist and which mode they use.

**Fix:** Update docs/agents.md line 37 to add `RA` to Architect, and add VS to the Audit row.

---

### 2. docs/agents.md — Menu Missing VS, RA, and Compose-Mode

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/agents.md` lines 50-73

The "Menu" section in the agents reference shows an illustrative menu that is materially incomplete. It shows the SF/AN/BS/CS/QS/SS/US/AS/TS/EX groups but has no mention of VS or RA anywhere. The menu example will be the first thing users read when they open the agents reference, and it makes these two workflows invisible.

Specifically:
- There is no ARCHITECTURE VERIFICATION group (VS and RA)
- There is no mention of compose-mode for SS

**Fix:** Add an ARCHITECTURE VERIFICATION group to the menu display in docs/agents.md with `[VS] Verify Stack` and `[RA] Refine Architecture`.

---

### 3. src/knowledge/overview.md — Workflow Count Stale at 12

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 9

```
The SKF module has 12 workflows containing 32 operational `data/` files...
```

This is loaded at runtime by Ferris via JiT loading. There are now 12 named workflows in the table (SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, VS, RA, EX) but the count remains "12 workflows" — this is only coincidentally accurate. The issue is in the knowledge map table below (line 27-34) which correctly lists only 12 knowledge files as of this version, but states "All 12" for progressive-capability.md and skill-lifecycle.md. Since these fragments are loaded JiT by Ferris, if Ferris loads overview.md and reads "12 workflows", that is consistent — but the knowledge map rows that show "All 12" are implicitly referring to the 12 workflows, not including VS and RA in the cross-cutting scope.

More precisely: the table at line 25-36 shows `progressive-capability.md` as applying to "All 12" workflows, but there are now 12 workflows including VS and RA. This is technically still correct (12 is 12) but for any future change this silent correspondence would break. The real issue is that `progressive-capability.md` and `skill-lifecycle.md` both state they cover "All 12" but VS and RA don't depend on capability tier (both state "No dependency on setup-forge or forge-tier" in their init steps). The "All 12" claim is therefore inaccurate for progressive-capability — VS and RA are tier-agnostic by design.

**Fix:** Update `overview.md` line 9 to acknowledge the correct count of 12 workflows (which is accidentally still 12 if you count them), but more importantly fix the "All 12" in the knowledge map rows for `progressive-capability.md` and `skill-lifecycle.md` to be accurate. VS and RA do not use progressive capability tiers. The Key Workflows column for `progressive-capability.md` should exclude VS and RA.

---

## IMPORTANT Issues

### 4. docs/getting-started.md — No Mention of VS, RA, or Compose-Mode

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/getting-started.md`

This file has a "Common Use Cases" section with 8 specific scenarios and a "First Steps" section with 3 use cases. None of them mention VS, RA, or compose-mode. There is no "I'm planning a new project and want to verify my tech stack works" use case.

Given that the CHANGELOG.md describes VS as a primary feature and the architecture-verification knowledge fragment describes it as a core workflow, its complete absence from getting-started.md means a key new capability is invisible to new users.

Contrast with the workflows.md which has a full `Architecture Verification Workflows` section. The docs are inconsistent — a key audience document (getting-started.md) is unaware of the new features.

**Fix:** Add a "Pre-code architecture verification" use case to `docs/getting-started.md` showing the `VS → RA → SS (compose-mode)` flow.

---

### 5. docs/examples.md — No VS, RA, or Compose-Mode Examples

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/examples.md`

The examples page shows 4 "Example Workflows" and 4 "Common Scenarios". None of them demonstrate VS, RA, or compose-mode. The page documents "Quick Skill", "Brownfield Platform", "Release Prep", and "Stack Skill" but has no scenario for "Greenfield with architecture verification" or "pre-code stack validation".

This is especially notable because the architecture-verification.md knowledge fragment was explicitly created to document this pipeline. The docs surface it in workflows.md but examples.md — the most pragmatic page for users — does not contain a single example of the new capability.

**Fix:** Add a "Pre-code Architecture Verification" example to `docs/examples.md` showing the `CS/QS ×N → VS → RA → SS (compose-mode)` workflow with a concrete scenario.

---

### 6. src/knowledge/overview.md — Knowledge Map Missing VS in progressive-capability Scope

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 27

The knowledge map shows `progressive-capability.md` applies to "All 12" workflows. But VS and RA both explicitly state in their init step files: "No dependency on setup-forge or forge-tier — this workflow operates on skill content only." These workflows are tier-agnostic by design. Including them in the "All 12" scope for progressive-capability is misleading to Ferris at runtime when it uses this map to decide which knowledge to load.

**Fix:** Change `progressive-capability.md`'s Key Workflows to "All 12 except VS, RA" or list the actual workflows.

---

### 7. src/knowledge/overview.md — "12 workflows" vs. "All 12" Implicit Count

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 9 and line 27-29

The knowledge overview states "12 workflows" in its rationale. The knowledge map says `progressive-capability.md` and `skill-lifecycle.md` apply to "All 12". These two counts are coincidentally consistent with the current state (there are exactly 12 workflows). However, `skill-lifecycle.md` explicitly covers VS and RA in its own content (line 174: "synthesized from all 12 workflow.md files (including VS, RA)"), yet the knowledge map shows "All 12" — which, if a reader misinterprets "12" from the count in the rationale as the old 10 (pre-VS/RA), creates confusion. This is a latent issue introduced by coincidence.

The "32 operational `data/` files" count is accurate (verified by counting).

---

### 8. Workflow Count Inconsistency: CHANGELOG.md vs. docs/workflows.md Build Priority Table

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/workflows.md` lines 228-234

The Build Priority table still shows the original phased build plan:
```
| 1a (Core Engine) | setup-forge, create-skill, test-skill, export-skill | ...
| 1b (Entry Points) | quick-skill, brief-skill, create-stack-skill | ...
| 1c (Verification) | verify-stack, refine-architecture | ...
| 2 (Lifecycle) | audit-skill, update-skill, analyze-source | ...
```

This table is a development artifact describing build order, not a user reference. Since all 12 workflows are now shipped, this table is misleading — it implies VS and RA are "1c" (not yet core) and that lifecycle workflows are "Phase 2" (possibly not yet available). A user reading this might conclude VS/RA or update-skill are not yet ready.

**Fix:** Remove or replace the Build Priority table in `docs/workflows.md` with a lifecycle/category table that reflects the shipped product (all 12 workflows available).

---

### 9. test/test-installation-components.js — Compose-Mode Data File Not Tested

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/test/test-installation-components.js` lines 183-218

The test at Suite 5 explicitly tests VS and RA step files and data files. For `create-stack-skill`, the test checks only the `steps` chain (no explicit data file test is included in the stepFileChains object for create-stack-skill). The new `compose-mode-rules.md` file that was added for SS compose-mode is not verified to exist.

Specifically, the test object at line 183 only has `verify-stack` and `refine-architecture` entries. The `create-stack-skill` workflow is tested by workflow.md existence (Test Suite 4, line 150-173) but its specific new data file `data/compose-mode-rules.md` is not covered.

**Fix:** Add `create-stack-skill` to the `stepFileChains` object with `data: ['compose-mode-rules.md', 'integration-patterns.md', 'manifest-patterns.md', 'stack-skill-template.md']`.

---

### 10. Validation Report Unfixed Issue: skfConfigFile in step-01-init.md

**Confidence: MEDIUM** (needs runtime verification)

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/validation-report.md` lines 383-387

The validation report says: "Must fix (1): Remove `skfConfigFile` from step-01-init.md frontmatter (unused variable)."

The actual `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-01-init.md` does NOT contain `skfConfigFile` in its frontmatter — that variable has been removed. This means the validation report is now stale/inaccurate since it still shows this as a "must fix" that has already been fixed.

This is a documentation inconsistency: the validation report says there is an open must-fix, but the issue is already resolved. A reader of the validation-report.md will be confused.

**Fix:** Update `create-stack-skill/validation-report.md` to mark the `skfConfigFile` issue as resolved.

---

### 11. docs/workflows.md — SS Description Omits Compose-Mode Triggers

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/workflows.md` lines 86-97

The Stack Skill (SS) entry reads:

```
**Key Steps:** Detect manifests → Rank dependencies → Scope confirmation → Parallel extract → Detect integrations → Compile stack → Generate references
```

These are exclusively the code-mode steps. Compose-mode has a completely different flow (scan existing skills → sort by architecture layer → user confirms → synthesize integrations → compile → validate → report). The SS description is missing any mention of compose-mode and gives users no indication that this workflow can operate without a codebase.

**Fix:** Update the SS entry in `docs/workflows.md` to note both modes and add a compose-mode key steps description.

---

## LOWER CONFIDENCE Issues

### 12. Cross-Reference in SS Workflow Plan — Wrong Export Skill Trigger Code

**Confidence: HIGH** (documentation inconsistency in plan artifact)

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/workflow-plan-create-stack-skill.md` line 360

The plan says: "Recommend next workflows: [TS] Test Skill, `[ES] Export Skill`"

The correct trigger code for Export Skill is `[EX]`, not `[ES]`. The actual step-09-report.md correctly uses `[EX]`. This is a stale reference in the workflow plan artifact.

**Fix:** Update workflow-plan-create-stack-skill.md line 360 from `[ES] Export Skill` to `[EX] Export Skill`.

---

**Confidence: MEDIUM**

The VS workflow operates directly on skill confidence data throughout its analysis passes.

**Fix:** Add VS to the Key Workflows column for `confidence-tiers.md` in overview.md.

---

**Confidence: MEDIUM**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/module-help.csv` line 13

The RA entry uses `output_folder` as the output-location column, while VS uses `forge_data_folder`. The `output_folder` config variable is RA-specific (it must be configured in config.yaml per RA's step-01 validation check). However, `output_folder` is not documented as a standard SKF variable in `docs/getting-started.md` configuration table (which only shows `skills_output_folder` and `forge_data_folder`).

This creates a gap: a user reading module-help.csv and getting-started.md will not know what `output_folder` is or how to configure it.

**Fix:** Either document `output_folder` in the getting-started.md configuration table, or update the RA module-help.csv entry to clarify the variable.

**Fix:** Add a "Pre-code Architecture Verification" example to `docs/examples.md` showing the `CS/QS ×N → VS → RA → SS (compose-mode)` workflow with a concrete scenario.

---

### 6. src/knowledge/overview.md — Knowledge Map Missing VS in progressive-capability Scope

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 27

The knowledge map shows `progressive-capability.md` applies to "All 12" workflows. But VS and RA both explicitly state in their init step files: "No dependency on setup-forge or forge-tier — this workflow operates on skill content only." These workflows are tier-agnostic by design. Including them in the "All 12" scope for progressive-capability is misleading to Ferris at runtime when it uses this map to decide which knowledge to load.

**Fix:** Change `progressive-capability.md`'s Key Workflows to "All 12 except VS, RA" or list the actual workflows.

---

### 7. src/knowledge/overview.md — "12 workflows" vs. "All 12" Implicit Count

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 9 and line 27-29

The knowledge overview states "12 workflows" in its rationale. The knowledge map says `progressive-capability.md` and `skill-lifecycle.md` apply to "All 12". These two counts are coincidentally consistent with the current state (there are exactly 12 workflows). However, `skill-lifecycle.md` explicitly covers VS and RA in its own content (line 174: "synthesized from all 12 workflow.md files (including VS, RA)"), yet the knowledge map shows "All 12" — which, if a reader misinterprets "12" from the count in the rationale as the old 10 (pre-VS/RA), creates confusion. This is a latent issue introduced by coincidence.

The "32 operational `data/` files" count is accurate (verified by counting).

---

### 8. Workflow Count Inconsistency: CHANGELOG.md vs. docs/workflows.md Build Priority Table

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/workflows.md` lines 228-234

The Build Priority table still shows the original phased build plan:
```
| 1a (Core Engine) | setup-forge, create-skill, test-skill, export-skill | ...
| 1b (Entry Points) | quick-skill, brief-skill, create-stack-skill | ...
| 1c (Verification) | verify-stack, refine-architecture | ...
| 2 (Lifecycle) | audit-skill, update-skill, analyze-source | ...
```

This table is a development artifact describing build order, not a user reference. Since all 12 workflows are now shipped, this table is misleading — it implies VS and RA are "1c" (not yet core) and that lifecycle workflows are "Phase 2" (possibly not yet available). A user reading this might conclude VS/RA or update-skill are not yet ready.

**Fix:** Remove or replace the Build Priority table in `docs/workflows.md` with a lifecycle/category table that reflects the shipped product (all 12 workflows available).

---

### 9. test/test-installation-components.js — Compose-Mode Data File Not Tested

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/test/test-installation-components.js` lines 183-218

The test at Suite 5 explicitly tests VS and RA step files and data files. For `create-stack-skill`, the test checks only the `steps` chain (no explicit data file test is included in the stepFileChains object for create-stack-skill). The new `compose-mode-rules.md` file that was added for SS compose-mode is not verified to exist.

Specifically, the test object at line 183 only has `verify-stack` and `refine-architecture` entries. The `create-stack-skill` workflow is tested by workflow.md existence (Test Suite 4, line 150-173) but its specific new data file `data/compose-mode-rules.md` is not covered.

**Fix:** Add `create-stack-skill` to the `stepFileChains` object with `data: ['compose-mode-rules.md', 'integration-patterns.md', 'manifest-patterns.md', 'stack-skill-template.md']`.

---

### 10. Validation Report Unfixed Issue: skfConfigFile in step-01-init.md

**Confidence: MEDIUM** (needs runtime verification)

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/validation-report.md` lines 383-387

The validation report says: "Must fix (1): Remove `skfConfigFile` from step-01-init.md frontmatter (unused variable)."

The actual `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-01-init.md` does NOT contain `skfConfigFile` in its frontmatter — that variable has been removed. This means the validation report is now stale/inaccurate since it still shows this as a "must fix" that has already been fixed.

This is a documentation inconsistency: the validation report says there is an open must-fix, but the issue is already resolved. A reader of the validation-report.md will be confused.

**Fix:** Update `create-stack-skill/validation-report.md` to mark the `skfConfigFile` issue as resolved.

---

### 11. docs/workflows.md — SS Description Omits Compose-Mode Triggers

**Confidence: HIGH**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/workflows.md` lines 86-97

The Stack Skill (SS) entry reads:

```
**Key Steps:** Detect manifests → Rank dependencies → Scope confirmation → Parallel extract → Detect integrations → Compile stack → Generate references
```

These are exclusively the code-mode steps. Compose-mode has a completely different flow (scan existing skills → sort by architecture layer → user confirms → synthesize integrations → compile → validate → report). The SS description is missing any mention of compose-mode and gives users no indication that this workflow can operate without a codebase.

**Fix:** Update the SS entry in `docs/workflows.md` to note both modes and add a compose-mode key steps description.

---

## LOWER CONFIDENCE Issues

### 12. Cross-Reference in SS Workflow Plan — Wrong Export Skill Trigger Code

**Confidence: HIGH** (documentation inconsistency in plan artifact)

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/workflow-plan-create-stack-skill.md` line 360

The plan says: "Recommend next workflows: [TS] Test Skill, `[ES] Export Skill`"

The correct trigger code for Export Skill is `[EX]`, not `[ES]`. The actual step-09-report.md correctly uses `[EX]`. This is a stale reference in the workflow plan artifact.

**Fix:** Update workflow-plan-create-stack-skill.md line 360 from `[ES] Export Skill` to `[EX] Export Skill`.

---

### 13. knowledge/overview.md Knowledge Map — VS Missing from confidence-tiers.md Scope

**Confidence: MEDIUM**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/knowledge/overview.md` line 26

The knowledge map shows `confidence-tiers.md` applies to "AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, EX, RA". VS is missing from this list.

VS actively uses confidence tiers: step-01-init.md reads `confidence_tier` from each skill's metadata.json (line 83 of that step), and step-03-integrations.md reads `confidence_tier` from metadata.json to assess evidence quality (line 92). The VS workflow operates directly on skill confidence data throughout its analysis passes.

**Fix:** Add VS to the Key Workflows column for `confidence-tiers.md` in overview.md.

---

### 14. module-help.csv — RA Output Location Uses Generic "output_folder"

**Confidence: MEDIUM**

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/module-help.csv` line 13

The RA entry uses `output_folder` as the output-location column, while VS uses `forge_data_folder`. The `output_folder` config variable is RA-specific (it must be configured in config.yaml per RA's step-01 validation check). However, `output_folder` is not documented as a standard SKF variable in `docs/getting-started.md` configuration table (which only shows `skills_output_folder` and `forge_data_folder`).

This creates a gap: a user reading module-help.csv and getting-started.md will not know what `output_folder` is or how to configure it.

**Fix:** Either document `output_folder` in the getting-started.md configuration table, or update the RA module-help.csv entry to clarify the variable.

content.text:
This is a real bug. Step-06-report.md line 121 says for FEASIBLE: "requirements are fulfilled **or partially fulfilled** (or no PRD was provided)". But step-05-synthesize.md's logic says "Partially Fulfilled items → CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE". If the verdict is FEASIBLE, there cannot be any "Partially Fulfilled" items. The description in step-06-report.md is wrong — it incorrectly includes "partially fulfilled" in the FEASIBLE description. This creates a logical contradiction: the user would see "FEASIBLE" verdict but the description says partially fulfilled is OK for FEASIBLE, when the synthesize step would have returned CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE for that case.

Now I have a complete picture. Let me compile the full report.

---

## Deep Review Report: Bugs, Logic Errors, and Functional Correctness

This review covers all 44 changed files across commits 3275418 through b44b7a8. Issues are ordered by severity.

---

## CRITICAL Severity

### ISSUE 1: compose-mode in step-03 never populates `confirmed_dependencies` before the gate

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-03-rank-and-confirm.md`
**Lines:** 55–65 (compose-mode branch), 153 (menu handler)
**Confidence:** HIGH

The compose-mode path in Section 1 says "All skills are included by default" and then instructs: "Skip to section 5 (Present MENU OPTIONS)." Section 5's menu handler reads:

```
- IF C: Store confirmed_dependencies, then load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
```

The problem: the compose-mode branch never sets `confirmed_dependencies` to any value before jumping to Section 5. Section 4 ("Process User Response") is the only place where `confirmed_dependencies` gets assigned (from the above-threshold list, modifications, or custom list) — and that entire section is bypassed. When the user presses C, the agent is instructed to store `confirmed_dependencies`, but the variable has no defined value in the compose-mode execution path.

Every downstream step (04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 09) references `confirmed_dependencies` as its input. With this variable undefined, the entire post-gate workflow operates on a null/undefined dependency list.

**Fix:** In the compose-mode branch of Section 1, explicitly add: "Set `confirmed_dependencies` = all `raw_dependencies` loaded from `{skills_output_folder}` in Step 02. User may modify this list before confirming at the gate."

---

### ISSUE 2: VS verdict format in compose-mode-rules.md is inconsistent with the actual VS report format

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/data/compose-mode-rules.md`
**Line:** 50
**Confidence:** HIGH

The Feasibility Report Integration section says:
```
Include the verdict in the integration evidence: `VS verdict: {pass|warn|fail}`
```

But the VS workflow produces verdicts with values `FEASIBLE`, `CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE`, and `NOT FEASIBLE` (defined in step-05-synthesize.md lines 61–74 and stored in the `overall_verdict` frontmatter field of the feasibility report). The `{pass|warn|fail}` schema does not exist anywhere in the VS output format.

This is a data integrity bug: when compose-mode (step-05-detect-integrations.md line 76) reads the VS feasibility report and includes the verdict in integration evidence, it will be looking for `pass|warn|fail` values that are never written — and will embed garbage or empty values into the generated stack skill.

**Fix:** Change line 50 to: `VS verdict: {FEASIBLE|CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE|NOT FEASIBLE}` to match the actual VS output schema.

---

### ISSUE 3: Step-06-report.md FEASIBLE description contradicts the synthesize verdict logic

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/verify-stack/steps-c/step-06-report.md`
**Line:** 121
**Confidence:** HIGH

The FEASIBLE message displayed to the user says:
```
"Your stack is verified. All technologies are covered, integrations are compatible, 
and requirements are fulfilled or partially fulfilled (or no PRD was provided)."
```

But step-05-synthesize.md line 69 explicitly states that "Partially Fulfilled items (but none Not Addressed) → CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE." If the verdict is FEASIBLE, there cannot be any Partially Fulfilled requirements — the synthesize logic would have set CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE instead. The description in step-06-report.md incorrectly teaches the user that "partially fulfilled" is acceptable for FEASIBLE, which undermines confidence in the verdict system.

**Fix:** Change line 121 to: "requirements are all fulfilled (or requirements pass was skipped)" — removing "or partially fulfilled."

---

## IMPORTANT Severity

### ISSUE 4: Duplicate step number `4.` in compose-mode numbered list creates ambiguous execution order

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-05-detect-integrations.md`
**Lines:** 72–73
**Confidence:** HIGH

The compose-mode numbered list reads:
```
1. Load `{composeModeRules}` for integration evidence format rules
2. If `{architecture_doc_path}` is null or not available: Skip directly...
3. Load the architecture document from `{architecture_doc_path}`
4. Use prose-based co-mention analysis: find paragraphs mentioning 2+ confirmed skill names
4. For each detected integration pair:        ← DUPLICATE 4
```

Both steps are numbered `4.`. An agent following these instructions encounters two separate actions labeled as the same step. The second `4.` should be `5.`, and the entire list should be renumbered to close the block properly before "All integration evidence inherits confidence tiers from the source skills."

**Fix:** Renumber the second `4.` to `5.` and any subsequent items accordingly.

---

### ISSUE 5: compose-mode scope modification loop in step-03 has no skills table to redisplay

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-03-rank-and-confirm.md`
**Lines:** 55–65, 154
**Confidence:** HIGH

In compose-mode, the skills table (sorted by architectural layer) is presented in Section 1, and then execution skips directly to Section 5. Section 5's "Any other" (non-C) handling says "Process as scope modification, redisplay updated list, then [Redisplay Menu Options]." The redisplay loop targets Section 5 (`#5-present-menu-options`), but that section contains only the menu — not the skills table. After any scope modification, the user would see the menu again but not the updated skills list.

This is a UX/flow bug: the user cannot see what the updated scope looks like after modification because the table lives in Section 1 (which was skipped) and the redisplay loop only restarts at Section 5.

**Fix:** Add the skills table display to Section 5 itself (or create an explicit "redisplay table" action in the scope modification branch), so it is visible during the redisplay loop.

---

### ISSUE 6: step-05-synthesize.md redundantly re-sets integration counts already set by step-03

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/verify-stack/steps-c/step-05-synthesize.md`
**Line:** 148
**Confidence:** MEDIUM

Step-03-integrations.md (line 155) instructs: "Set `integrations_verified`, `integrations_plausible`, `integrations_risky`, `integrations_blocked` counts" in the frontmatter. Step-05-synthesize.md (line 148) then also instructs: "Set `integrations_verified`, `integrations_plausible`, `integrations_risky`, `integrations_blocked` to match the counts from Step 03."

This duplication is not incorrect in terms of final state, but it is misleading: the instruction in Step 05 implies these fields are not yet set when they already are. More critically, if the agent re-writes these values in Step 05 with slightly different counts (e.g., due to context drift across steps), the report frontmatter will have incorrect values. The source of truth should be defined once.

**Fix:** Remove the duplicate field-setting instruction from step-05-synthesize.md line 148, or rephrase it as "Verify frontmatter integration counts match Step 03 findings" (read-only check, not a write).

---

### ISSUE 7: `previous_report` delta feature has no input path — users cannot actually trigger it

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/verify-stack/steps-c/step-05-synthesize.md`
**Lines:** 100–115
**Confidence:** MEDIUM

Step-05's delta feature checks "if a `previous_report` path was recorded in the current report's frontmatter." The step notes that "the delta feature requires the user to have saved a copy before re-running [VS]." content.text:
This is a real bug. Step-06-report.md line 121 says for FEASIBLE: "requirements are fulfilled **or partially fulfilled** (or no PRD was provided)". But step-05-synthesize.md's logic says "Partially Fulfilled items → CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE". If the verdict is FEASIBLE, there cannot be any "Partially Fulfilled" items. The description in step-06-report.md is wrong — it incorrectly includes "partially fulfilled" in the FEASIBLE description. This creates a logical contradiction: the user would see "FEASIBLE" verdict but the description says partially fulfilled is OK for FEASIBLE, when the synthesize step would have returned CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE for that case.

Now I have a complete picture. Let me compile the full report.

---

## Deep Review Report: Bugs, Logic Errors, and Functional Correctness

This review covers all 44 changed files across commits 3275418 through b44b7a8. Issues are ordered by severity.

---

## CRITICAL Severity

### ISSUE 1: compose-mode in step-03 never populates `confirmed_dependencies` before the gate

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/steps-c/step-03-rank-and-confirm.md`
**Lines:** 55–65 (compose-mode branch), 153 (menu handler)
**Confidence:** HIGH

The compose-mode path in Section 1 says "All skills are included by default" and then instructs: "Skip to section 5 (Present MENU OPTIONS)." Section 5's menu handler reads:

```
- IF C: Store confirmed_dependencies, then load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
```

The problem: the compose-mode branch never sets `confirmed_dependencies` to any value before jumping to Section 5. Section 4 ("Process User Response") is the only place where `confirmed_dependencies` gets assigned (from the above-threshold list, modifications, or custom list) — and that entire section is bypassed. When the user presses C, the agent is instructed to store `confirmed_dependencies`, but the variable has no defined value in the compose-mode execution path.

Every downstream step (04, 05, 06, 07, 08, 09) references `confirmed_dependencies` as its input. With this variable undefined, the entire post-gate workflow operates on a null/undefined dependency list.

**Fix:** In the compose-mode branch of Section 1, explicitly add: "Set `confirmed_dependencies` = all `raw_dependencies` loaded from `{skills_output_folder}` in Step 02. User may modify this list before confirming at the gate."

---

### ISSUE 2: VS verdict format in compose-mode-rules.md is inconsistent with the actual VS report format

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/create-stack-skill/data/compose-mode-rules.md`
**Line:** 50
**Confidence:** HIGH

The Feasibility Report Integration section says:
```
Include the verdict in the integration evidence: `VS verdict: {pass|warn|fail}`
```

But the VS workflow produces verdicts with values `FEASIBLE`, `CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE`, and `NOT FEASIBLE` (defined in step-05-synthesize.md lines 61–74 and stored in the `overall_verdict` frontmatter field of the feasibility report). The `{pass|warn|fail}` schema does not exist anywhere in the VS output format.

This is a data integrity bug: when compose-mode (step-05-detect-integrations.md line 76) reads the VS feasibility report and includes the verdict in integration evidence, it will be looking for `pass|warn|fail` values that are never written — and will embed garbage or empty values into the generated stack skill.

**Fix:** Change line 50 to: `VS verdict: {FEASIBLE|CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE|NOT FEASIBLE}` to match the actual VS output schema.

---

### ISSUE 3: Step-06-report.md FEASIBLE description contradicts the synthesize verdict logic

**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/workflows/verify-stack/steps-c/step-06-report.md`
**Line:** 121
**Confidence:** HIGH

The FEASIBLE message displayed to the user says:
```
"Your stack is verified. All technologies are covered, integrations are compatible, 
and requirements are fulfilled or partially fulfilled (or no PRD was provided)."
```

But step-05-synthesize.md line 69 explicitly states that "Partially Fulfilled items (but none Not Addressed) → CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE." If the verdict is FEASIBLE, there cannot be any Partially Fulfilled requirements — the synthesize logic would have set CONDITIONALLY FEASIBLE instead. The description in step-06-report.md incorrectly teaches the user that "partially fulfilled" is acceptable for FEASIBLE, which undermines confidence in the verdict system.

**Fix:** Change line 121 to: "requirements are all fulfilled (or requirements pass was skipped)" — removing "or partially fulfilled."

---

4. Use prose-based co-mention analysis: find paragraphs mentioning 2+ confirmed skill names
4. For each detected integration pair:        ← DUPLICATE 4
```

Both steps are numbered `4.`. An agent following these instructions encounters two separate actions labeled as the same step.
